skill.name: semantic-scholar-library-feed
skill.description: Work with an authenticated Semantic Scholar account to inspect Research Feeds, manage Library folders, import cookies, and resolve paper identifiers.
skill.summary: Inspect Semantic Scholar feeds and Library folders with an authenticated local CLI.
skill.author: zjsxply

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-reported many Markdown backtick and base64 examples, which are mostly false positives. AI review confirmed real high-risk behavior: authenticated cookies can be sent to a caller-supplied page URL, and the skill stores plaintext session cookies locally. No evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Cookie Leak Through User-Controlled Page URL
high_findings.description: The ssr-dump and feed-crawl commands accept --page-url and pass it to fetch_html. fetch_html adds the saved Cookie header without validating that the target host is Semantic Scholar. A malicious or mistaken URL could receive sid and s2 cookies.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly combines a caller-supplied URL with authenticated Cookie headers. No origin check is present before the request is sent.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext Semantic Scholar Cookie Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally saves Semantic Scholar session cookies and a raw Cookie header to local plaintext files. This enables the workflow, but local file exposure could compromise the user account session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage paths and write operations are explicit. The risk is local exposure of account cookies, not hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Library Write Operation
medium_findings.description: The folder-add command sends an authenticated POST to the Library entries bulk endpoint. This is an intended feature, but it can modify private Library folders if invoked with valid cookies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The POST request body and authenticated endpoint are clear. It is legitimate functionality, but it has user-account side effects.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local File Read and Write Options
medium_findings.description: Several CLI arguments read user-supplied files or write output to user-supplied paths. This is expected for a CLI, but agents should avoid untrusted paths and sensitive shared directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and user controlled. They are normal CLI behavior, but they increase misuse impact.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are shell commands shown in Markdown code fences or inline code. They invoke the bundled Python CLI and are not Ruby backtick execution inside running code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples, not executable Ruby or shell interpolation in source code.

low_findings.title: Base64 Decode Is SSR Parsing, Not Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The base64 operation decodes Semantic Scholar SSR DATA before URL decoding and JSON parsing. It is not used for encryption, hashing, or trust decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code and reference text both describe decoding page data. No cryptographic security claim is made.

low_findings.title: Expected Semantic Scholar Network Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Semantic Scholar and its Graph API, which match the skill purpose. Network use is expected, but authenticated requests need origin validation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains are first-party Semantic Scholar endpoints for the advertised research workflow. The concern is not the hardcoded domains themselves.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Cookie Leak Through User-Controlled Page URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The ssr-dump and feed-crawl commands accept --page-url and pass it to fetch_html. fetch_html adds the saved Cookie header without validating that the target host is Semantic Scholar. A malicious or mistaken URL could receive sid and s2 cookies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly combines a caller-supplied URL with authenticated Cookie headers. No origin check is present before the request is sent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Semantic Scholar Cookie Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill intentionally saves Semantic Scholar session cookies and a raw Cookie header to local plaintext files. This enables the workflow, but local file exposure could compromise the user account session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The storage paths and write operations are explicit. The risk is local exposure of account cookies, not hidden exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Library Write Operation
dangerous_patterns.description: The folder-add command sends an authenticated POST to the Library entries bulk endpoint. This is an intended feature, but it can modify private Library folders if invoked with valid cookies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The POST request body and authenticated endpoint are clear. It is legitimate functionality, but it has user-account side effects.

content.value_statement: Researchers need repeatable access to Semantic Scholar feeds and private Library folders. This skill provides a CLI workflow for authenticated export, folder updates, and paper ID resolution.
actual_capabilities: Imports Semantic Scholar cookies from a browser curl command or raw Cookie header.
Checks cookie health and masks saved cookie headers in summaries.
Fetches and decodes authenticated recommendation page SSR data.
Crawls Research Feed windows and writes incremental local output.
Lists private Library folder entries and adds papers to selected folders.
Resolves arXiv, DOI, and Corpus identifiers through Semantic Scholar Graph batch lookup.
limitations: Requires user-provided authenticated Semantic Scholar cookies.
Does not perform browser login or manage account credentials directly.
Stores cookie material in local plaintext files.
Uses observed Semantic Scholar endpoints that may change without notice.

use_cases.title: Export a Research Feed
use_cases.description: Crawl authenticated recommendation windows and save a local feed snapshot for review.

use_cases.title: Audit Library Folders
use_cases.description: List folder entries, compare them with a target bibliography, and identify missing papers.

use_cases.title: Resolve Paper Identifiers
use_cases.description: Convert arXiv, DOI, or Corpus identifiers into Semantic Scholar paper records.

prompt_templates.title: Check Cookie Store
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether my Semantic Scholar cookie store exists and tell me which cookies are missing.

prompt_templates.title: Export Feed Snapshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Use my saved Semantic Scholar cookies to crawl my Research Feed and save an incremental local snapshot.

prompt_templates.title: Sync Folder From Bibliography
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare my bibliography identifiers with a Semantic Scholar Library folder, then list papers that need to be added.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Feed Pagination
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the SSR recommendation data, explain the pagination state, and identify why the feed crawl stopped.

output_examples.input: Check my Semantic Scholar cookie status.
output_examples.output: The cookie store exists. Required cookies are present. The saved header is masked before display.

output_examples.input: Export my Research Feed to a local file.
output_examples.output: The feed crawl saved each window as it completed. The summary reports window count, paper count, and stop reason.

output_examples.input: Resolve these arXiv IDs and prepare folder additions.
output: Resolved matching Semantic Scholar paper records.
Compared them with existing folder entries.
Prepared only missing papers for review before adding.

best_practices: Use only fresh cookies copied from your own authenticated Semantic Scholar session.
Keep cookie files outside shared folders and remove them after sensitive work.
Review folder additions with dry-run output before making authenticated writes.
anti_patterns: Do not paste Semantic Scholar cookies into public chats, tickets, or shared logs.
Do not use arbitrary page URLs with authenticated feed commands.
Do not trust title-only paper matching without exact title verification.

faq.question: Does this skill log in to Semantic Scholar?
faq.answer: No. It requires the user to provide cookies from an existing authenticated browser session.

faq.question: Where are cookies stored?
faq.answer: By default, cookies are stored under the local .auth directory in the user home folder.

faq.question: Can it modify my Semantic Scholar Library?
faq.answer: Yes. The folder-add command can add papers to selected Library folders when valid cookies are present.

faq.question: Can it export private Research Feed data?
faq.answer: Yes. It can fetch authenticated recommendation data and save local feed snapshots.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The CLI handles sensitive account cookies and can send them with authenticated requests.
